question_bank_items.prompt: In central London, which feature is the most useful broad north/south orientation line?
question_bank_items.explanation: The River Thames is the clearest central London orientation feature and separates many north-bank and south-bank routes.
question_bank_items.tip: When uncertain, place the Thames first, then locate the destination north or south of it.

incorrectExplanations.M25: The M25 is an orbital motorway outside central London, not a central orientation line.
incorrectExplanations.Regent's Canal only: The canal can help locally, but it is not the main central London divider.
incorrectExplanations.Piccadilly line: Tube lines are useful references, but road-map orientation should start with visible geography.

question_bank_items.prompt: Which road corridor links Marylebone Road, Euston Road, Pentonville Road, and City Road?
question_bank_items.explanation: These roads form the A501 / Inner Ring Road corridor across north central London.
question_bank_items.tip: Learn the A501 as a driver orientation spine from west to east across north central London.

incorrectExplanations.A3: The A3 is a different south-west route corridor.
incorrectExplanations.South Circular: The South Circular is well south of this central corridor.
incorrectExplanations.A10 only: The A10 links north-south routes, but it is not the full west-east corridor named here.

question_bank_items.prompt: Which Thames bridge connects Westminster with the St Thomas' Hospital / Waterloo area?
question_bank_items.explanation: Westminster Bridge crosses the Thames between Parliament Square and the St Thomas' Hospital side.
question_bank_items.tip: Pair each bridge with the nearest landmark on both banks.

incorrectExplanations.Tower Bridge: Tower Bridge is much farther east by the Tower of London.
incorrectExplanations.Chelsea Bridge: Chelsea Bridge is west of Westminster.
incorrectExplanations.Putney Bridge: Putney Bridge is in west London, away from the Westminster hospital area.

question_bank_items.prompt: Which two mainline stations sit close together around King's Cross Road and Euston Road?
question_bank_items.explanation: King's Cross and St Pancras are adjacent major termini and are often treated as one transport hub for local orientation.
question_bank_items.tip: Treat King's Cross St Pancras as a major north-central anchor when reading route questions.

incorrectExplanations.Victoria and Waterloo: These stations are on different sides of Westminster and the river.
incorrectExplanations.Paddington and Marylebone: These are both west/north-west central, but not the King's Cross Road hub.
incorrectExplanations.London Bridge and Blackfriars: These are farther south/east around the Thames and City approaches.

question_bank_items.prompt: University College Hospital is most closely associated with which central London road corridor?
question_bank_items.explanation: University College Hospital is near Euston Road and Tottenham Court Road.
question_bank_items.tip: Hospitals often need both a landmark and a road-corridor memory cue.

incorrectExplanations.King's Road: King's Road is in Chelsea, not the UCLH area.
incorrectExplanations.Commercial Road: Commercial Road is in east London.
incorrectExplanations.Old Kent Road: Old Kent Road is south-east of central London.

question_bank_items.prompt: The British Museum is in which central London area?
question_bank_items.explanation: The British Museum is in Bloomsbury, close to Great Russell Street.
question_bank_items.tip: Link Bloomsbury with museums, hospitals, universities, and garden squares.

incorrectExplanations.Docklands: Docklands is east of the City, not the British Museum area.
incorrectExplanations.Battersea: Battersea is south-west of central London.

question_bank_items.prompt: A route looks shortest on the map, but the first street is one-way against you. What is the best judgement?
question_bank_items.explanation: The shortest visible line is not valid if it ignores traffic direction. A legal, driveable alternative is needed.
question_bank_items.tip: In route planning, first check that each chosen road can be driven in the intended direction.

options: Use the street anyway because it is shorter
Ignore one-way arrows on short roads
Stop planning and use only the destination road

incorrectExplanations.Use the street anyway because it is shor: A route must be lawful and driveable, not only short.
incorrectExplanations.Ignore one-way arrows on short roads: One-way restrictions still matter on short streets.
incorrectExplanations.Stop planning and use only the destinati: The route must still connect the start to the destination.

question_bank_items.prompt: On a north-up map, if a destination is to the right of your start point, it is generally in which direction?
question_bank_items.explanation: On a north-up map, east is to the right and west is to the left.
question_bank_items.tip: Before tracing roads, confirm north-up orientation and read east/west consistently.

incorrectExplanations.West: West is normally to the left on a north-up map.
incorrectExplanations.South: South is normally down the page.
incorrectExplanations.North-west: North-west would be up and left, not simply right.

question_bank_items.prompt: When using a printed street atlas, what is the sensible sequence for finding a named street?
question_bank_items.explanation: Use the street index to find the page/grid reference, then locate that grid square on the map page.
question_bank_items.tip: Do not scan a whole atlas page before using the index and grid.

options: Compass rose, scale bar, page edge
Nearest park, nearest station, road colour

incorrectExplanations.Compass rose, scale bar, page edge: Those help orientation and distance, but not first lookup of a named street.
incorrectExplanations.Nearest park, nearest station, road colo: Landmarks can help after locating the page/grid reference.
incorrectExplanations.Page number only, then guess: The grid reference avoids guessing within a dense road network.

question_bank_items.prompt: A passenger asks for a hospital entrance on a busy one-way street. What should a driver prioritise when planning the final approach?
question_bank_items.explanation: The final approach should be safe, legal, and close to the correct entrance, accounting for one-way and stopping restrictions.
question_bank_items.tip: For passenger scenarios, think beyond the destination name and plan the usable arrival side.

options: A safe legal approach to the correct entrance
The shortest line drawn across the map
Any nearby street with the same postcode
The first road with a bus stop
payload.correctAnswer: A safe legal approach to the correct entrance

incorrectExplanations.The shortest line drawn across the map: The shortest visual line may ignore access, legality, or the correct entrance.
incorrectExplanations.Any nearby street with the same postcode: Nearby is not enough if the passenger needs a specific entrance.
incorrectExplanations.The first road with a bus stop: A bus stop does not automatically make a safe or legal private-hire drop-off.

question_bank_items.explanation: Paddington is a major west-central London terminus around Praed Street.
question_bank_items.tip: Use Edgware Road, Bayswater, and Hyde Park as west-central orientation anchors.

payload.acceptedAreaDescription: The main Paddington station footprint and immediate entrance area.

question_bank_items.explanation: Victoria station is close to Westminster, Buckingham Palace Road, and Victoria Street.
question_bank_items.tip: Victoria is a useful west Westminster anchor for passenger routes.

payload.acceptedAreaDescription: The station concourse and immediate entrances around Victoria.

question_bank_items.explanation: St Thomas' Hospital is on the south bank by Westminster Bridge.
question_bank_items.tip: Find Parliament and Westminster Bridge, then look directly across the river.

payload.acceptedAreaDescription: The St Thomas' Hospital campus beside Westminster Bridge.

question_bank_items.explanation: Tower Bridge crosses the Thames just east of the Tower of London.
question_bank_items.tip: Do not confuse Tower Bridge with London Bridge, which is farther west.

payload.acceptedAreaDescription: The bridge deck and immediate approaches.

question_bank_items.explanation: The British Museum is in Bloomsbury near Great Russell Street.
question_bank_items.tip: Use Russell Square, Holborn, and Tottenham Court Road as nearby anchors.

payload.acceptedAreaDescription: The British Museum block and immediate entrances.

question_bank_items.explanation: Oxford Circus is the junction of Oxford Street and Regent Street.
question_bank_items.tip: Use major shopping streets as route anchors, but check one-way and access restrictions nearby.

payload.acceptedAreaDescription: The Oxford Circus junction and station entrance area.

question_bank_items.prompt: Plan a direct route from King's Cross St Pancras to Euston station.
question_bank_items.explanation: This is a short central route that should stay close to the Euston Road corridor while respecting junctions and access.
question_bank_items.tip: Identify the westbound corridor first, then check the final station approach.

payload.title: King's Cross St Pancras to Euston station
payload.idealRouteDescription: Use the Euston Road corridor westbound from King's Cross St Pancras toward Euston station, checking junction positioning before the final approach.

question_bank_items.prompt: Plan a route from Euston station to the British Museum.
question_bank_items.explanation: This journey tests Bloomsbury orientation and the choice of connected streets south of Euston Road.
question_bank_items.tip: Work from Euston toward Bloomsbury and avoid drifting too far east toward King's Cross.

payload.title: Euston station to the British Museum
payload.idealRouteDescription: Use a clear south-east Bloomsbury route from Euston toward Great Russell Street and the British Museum.

question_bank_items.prompt: Plan a route from Russell Square to University College Hospital.
question_bank_items.explanation: This route checks local Bloomsbury road reading and the final approach to a hospital destination.
question_bank_items.tip: Find the hospital's road corridor first, then check the final approach side.

payload.title: Russell Square to University College Hospital
payload.idealRouteDescription: Work west from Russell Square toward the Tottenham Court Road / Euston Road hospital area using connected streets.
